The root cause was a single limit breach on the energy desk.
Because the limit was not checked, our exposure grew.
As a result, the margin call arrived the same afternoon.
The knock-on effect hit our liquidity by Wednesday.
One missed check led to a whole chain of consequences.

Hi Sergio, it is Marina. Before the committee meets, here is the short story behind Tuesday's loss. The root cause was a single limit breach on the energy desk. Because that limit was crossed and not checked, our exposure jumped, and as a result the margin call arrived the same afternoon. The loss was not huge on its own, but it amplified fast. Since two partner funds held the same position, the stress spread, and the knock-on effect hit our liquidity by Wednesday. So the impact was much wider than the first number suggested. Please write a clear cause-and-effect summary for the committee, and show how one breach led to the whole chain. Thanks, talk soon.

Hi Sergio, it is Paulo from the credit desk. A quick note on the fallout from Tuesday. The direct loss was small, but the ripple effect was the real problem. Because the two funds sold at the same time, prices fell further, and consequently our collateral lost value too. That is contagion in one line: one desk's trouble becomes everyone's trouble. Owing to the speed of it, we had no time to hedge. For the report, my advice is simple. Name the root cause first, then follow the effects in order, so the committee sees the whole chain and not just the final number. Copy me when the draft is ready. Thanks.

First, name the root cause of the loss. Then show the first effect it produced. Next, link the cause to the result with a connector such as as a result. After that, explain the knock-on effect on the wider desk. Finally, state the full impact and the lesson for next time.
